root.narration: Your point guard is being tightly defended and calls for a screen. You run up to set one. How do you set the screen properly?

choices.text: Plant both feet wide, stand still with your arms crossed against your chest, and let your teammate dribble right off your shoulder
Get in position but keep your feet narrow and lean a little toward the defender
Stick your hip or knee out while still moving to trip up the defender

outcome.headline: Brick wall screen!
outcome.explanation: You set your feet wide, kept your body still, and created a solid wall. The defender ran right into you and got stuck. Your point guard dribbled free for an open shot. That is a textbook legal screen!
outcome.whatToRemember: A legal screen means: feet planted, body still, arms in tight. You must be set before contact. Think of yourself as a brick wall that cannot move.

keyTerms.definition: When an offensive player stands still in the path of a defender to free up a teammate. Your feet must be set and you cannot move into the defender.

outcome.headline: The screen kind of worked
outcome.explanation: Your narrow stance made you easy to push aside, and leaning in almost got you called for a foul. The defender squeezed around you. Your teammate got a little space, but not much.
outcome.whatToRemember: Wide feet make you harder to move. Stay perfectly still and upright. A strong base is the key to a good screen.

outcome.headline: Illegal screen! Offensive foul!
outcome.explanation: The referee saw you moving and sticking your body out. That is called an illegal screen or a moving pick. It is an offensive foul and the other team gets the ball.
outcome.whatToRemember: You MUST be completely still when the defender makes contact. Sticking out hips, knees, or arms while moving is always a foul.
